content:
# Joshua Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 232-233 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) (LXX)
**Source Images:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_009_page_001.jpg`, `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_009_page_002.jpg`; `../../source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/JOS_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### The Kings Unite Against Israel (vv. 1-2)

**1** And it came to pass, when all the kings who were beyond the Jordan, in the hill country and in the lowland and on all the coast of the Great Sea toward Lebanon — the Hittite and the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite — heard,

**2** they gathered themselves together to fight against Joshua and against Israel, with one accord.

---

### The Gibeonites' Deception (vv. 3-13)

**3** But the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and to Ai,

**4** and they also acted with cunning, and they went and disguised themselves as envoys. And they took worn-out sacks on their donkeys, and wineskins, worn-out and torn and bound up,

**5** and worn-out and patched sandals on their feet, and worn-out garments upon them; and all the bread of their provision was dry and had become crumbled.

**6** And they went to Joshua, to the camp at Gilgal, and they said to him and to the men of Israel, "We have come from a far country; now therefore, cut a covenant with us."

**7** And the men of Israel said to the Hivites, "Perhaps you are dwelling in our midst; how then can we cut a covenant with you?"

**8** And they said to Joshua, "We are your servants." And Joshua said to them, "Who are you, and from where do you come?"

**9** And they said to him, "Your servants have come from a very far country, because of the name of YHWH your God; for we have heard the report of him, and all that he did in Egypt,

**10** and all that he did to the two kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan — to Sihon king of Heshbon, and to Og king of Bashan, who was in Ashtaroth.

**11** And our elders and all the inhabitants of our country spoke to us, saying, 'Take provision in your hand for the journey, and go to meet them, and say to them, "We are your servants; now therefore, cut a covenant with us."'

**12** This our bread — we took it hot as our provision from our houses on the day we set out to go to you; but now, behold, it is dry and has become crumbled.

**13** And these wineskins which we filled were new, and behold, they are torn; and these our garments and our sandals are worn out from the very long journey."

---

### Israel Makes a Covenant Without Seeking YHWH (vv. 14-15)

**14** And the men took some of their provision, but they did not ask at the mouth of YHWH.

**15** And Joshua made peace with them and cut a covenant with them to let them live, and the leaders of the congregation swore an oath to them.

---

### The Deception Discovered (vv. 16-21)

**16** And it came to pass at the end of three days, after they had cut a covenant with them, that they heard that they were their neighbors and that they were dwelling in their midst.

**17** And the Sons of Israel set out and came to their cities on the third day. Now their cities were Gibeon, and Chephirah, and Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim.

**18** And the Sons of Israel did not strike them, because the leaders of the congregation had sworn to them by YHWH, the God of Israel. And all the congregation murmured against the leaders.

**19** And all the leaders said to all the congregation, "We have sworn to them by YHWH, the God of Israel, and now we are not able to touch them.

**20** This we will do to them: we will let them live, so that wrath will not be upon us because of the oath which we swore to them."

**21** And the leaders said to them, "Let them live." And they became cutters of wood and drawers of water for all the congregation, as the leaders had spoken to them.

---

### Joshua Confronts the Gibeonites (vv. 22-27)

**22** And Joshua summoned them and spoke to them, saying, "Why did you deceive us, saying, 'We are very far from you,' when you are dwelling in our midst?

**23** Now therefore, you are cursed, and none of you shall be cut off from being a servant — cutters of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God."

**24** And they answered Joshua and said, "Because it was surely told to your servants that YHWH your God commanded Moses his servant to give to you all the land and to destroy all the inhabitants of the land from before you; and we feared greatly for our lives because of you, and we did this thing.

**25** And now, behold, we are in your hand; as it is good and right in your eyes to do to us, do."

**26** And he did so to them, and he delivered them from the hand of the Sons of Israel, and they did not kill them.

**27** And Joshua made them on that day cutters of wood and drawers of water for the congregation and for the altar of YHWH, to this day, in the place which he should choose.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Transliteration & Divine Name
- **YHWH** (יהוה): Retained as the tetragrammaton throughout (vv. 9, 14, 18, 19, 24, 27), per LOGOS convention. Not rendered "the LORD."
- **Sons of Israel** (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, vv. 17, 18, 26): Retained literally per LOGOS policy; not "children of Israel" or "Israelites."

### Key Lexical Decisions
- **Cut a covenant** (כָּרַת בְּרִית, vv. 6, 7, 11, 15, 16): The Hebrew idiom is retained formally. The verb כָּרַת ("to cut") refers to the ritual of cutting an animal in covenant ceremony (cf. Gen 15:10, 18). LOGOS preserves the concrete imagery rather than flattening to "make a covenant."
- **Acted with cunning** (עָרְמָה, v. 4): From the root עָרַם, "to be shrewd/crafty." The same root describes the serpent in Gen 3:1 (עָרוּם). The LXX renders ἐπανουργήσαντο. This is morally ambiguous — "cunning" rather than simply "wisely" or "deceitfully."
- **Disguised themselves as envoys** (וַיִּצְטַיָּרוּ, v. 4): The Hitpael of צִיר is debated. The MT reading וַיִּצְטַיָּרוּ may derive from צִיר ("envoy, ambassador"), hence "made themselves out as envoys." Some read it as related to צֵידָה ("provision"), yielding "provisioned themselves." The Qere/Ketiv variation and LXX (ἐπεσιτίσαντο, "provisioned themselves") suggest textual uncertainty. LOGOS follows the MT consonantal text with the "envoy" reading, noting the alternative.
- **Murmured** (וַיִּלֹּנוּ, v. 18): The same verb (לוּן) used for Israel's wilderness complaints against Moses (Exod 15:24; 16:2; Num 14:2). This echo connects the congregation's response to a pattern of communal dissent.
- **Cutters of wood and drawers of water** (חֹטְבֵי עֵצִים וְשֹׁאֲבֵי מַיִם, vv. 21, 23, 27): A merism for menial labor. This phrase becomes the defining status of the Gibeonites. The terms recur three times in the chapter, framing their permanent role.
- **The place which he should choose** (בַּמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יִבְחָר, v. 27): A distinctly Deuteronomic phrase (cf. Deut 12:5, 11, 14, 18, 21, 26), pointing to the future centralized sanctuary. This links the Gibeonites' servitude to YHWH's eventual chosen worship site.

### Textual Notes
- **v. 1 — "beyond the Jordan"** (בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן): This phrase is perspectival. From the narrator's standpoint in the land, it could refer to either bank. Here in context it refers to the west of the Jordan — i.e., the kings within Canaan itself — as indicated by the geographic descriptions (hill country, lowland, coast).
- **v. 2 — "with one accord"** (פֶּה אֶחָד): Literally "one mouth." A Hebrew idiom for unanimity. The LXX renders ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτό ("together").
- **v. 4 — Ketiv/Qere**: The Leningrad Codex has a notable Ketiv/Qere at וַיִּצְטַיָּרוּ. The Ketiv (written form) suggests the צִיר root; the Qere may reflect a scribal tradition favoring the צֵידָה reading. The LXX (Vaticanus) supports the "provisioned" reading (ἐπεσιτίσαντο), while the MT consonantal text better supports "envoys."
- **v. 7 — "the Hivites"** (הַחִוִּי): The narrator reveals to the reader what the Gibeonites are concealing — they are Hivites, one of the seven nations designated for destruction (Deut 7:1). This creates dramatic irony: the men of Israel suspect the truth but proceed anyway.
- **v. 9 — Selective omission**: The Gibeonites carefully mention Egypt, Sihon, and Og — events from before the Jordan crossing — but omit Jericho and Ai, which would reveal knowledge of recent, nearby events. This is subtle narrative craft in the Hebrew text.
- **v. 14 — "did not ask at the mouth of YHWH"** (אֶת־פִּי יְהוָה לֹא שָׁאָלוּ): Literally "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask." This is the theological pivot of the chapter. The phrase implies inquiry through the Urim and Thummim or prophetic oracle (cf. Num 27:21). Israel's failure to consult YHWH is presented as the decisive error.
- **v. 17 — Four Gibeonite cities**: Gibeon (modern el-Jib), Chephirah (Tell Kefirah), Beeroth (possibly el-Bireh), and Kiriath-jearim (Tell el-Azhar/Deir el-Azar). All are located in the Benjamin hill country, approximately 6-8 miles northwest of Jerusalem. These cities later fall within the tribal allotment of Benjamin (Josh 18:25-28).
- **v. 23 — "you are cursed"** (אֲרוּרִים אַתֶּם): The passive participle of אָרַר. Joshua's curse echoes the curse of Canaan in Gen 9:25 (אָרוּר כְּנָעַן עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים, "cursed be Canaan, a servant of servants"), binding the Gibeonites to perpetual servitude. Yet the curse is tempered — they serve "the house of my God," not Israelite masters, giving their servitude a sacred character.
- **v. 27 — "to this day"** (עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה): An editorial note indicating the Gibeonites' role persisted to the time of the text's final composition. The Gibeonites (Nethinim, "those given") appear later in Ezra 2:43-58 and Nehemiah 7:46-60 as temple servants, suggesting continuity of this institution.

### LXX Divergences (Vaticanus)
- The LXX version of Joshua 9 has significant divergences from the MT, particularly in verse numbering and content. The LXX places the Gibeonite narrative after the altar-building episode of chapter 8 with some rearrangement.
- In v. 2, the LXX adds explicit reference to the region "this side of the Jordan" and includes slightly different geographic descriptions.
- The Vaticanus text generally supports the MT narrative but with the noted difference at v. 4 (ἐπεσιτίσαντο for וַיִּצְטַיָּרוּ) and occasional minor expansions.

### Structural Note
Joshua 9 is constructed as a narrative of failed discernment. The chapter pivots on v. 14: "they did not ask at the mouth of YHWH." Everything before v. 14 builds the deception; everything after reveals its consequences. The Gibeonites' cunning (עָרְמָה) contrasts with Israel's failure to seek divine wisdom — a theme that echoes Proverbs' valuation of both shrewdness and divine counsel. The chapter also establishes an important theological precedent: sworn oaths, even those obtained by deception, are binding before YHWH (cf. 2 Sam 21:1-9, where YHWH sends famine because Saul violated this covenant).
